question: Universal learning (UC) is embarked on Salesforce transformation journey, UC will decommission the legacy CRM system and migrate data to Salesforce. The data migration team asked for a recommendation to optimize the performance of the dataload to Salesforce. Which approach should used to meet the requirement?
optionA: Contact Salesforce support to schedule performance load.
optionB: Use Bulk API to process jobs in parallel mode.
optionC: Use Bulk API to processjobs in high performance mode.
optionD: Use Bulk API to process jobs in serial mode.
explanation: To optimize data-load performance for a full legacy migration, use the Bulk API in parallel mode, which processes multiple batches concurrently for maximum throughput (B). Serial mode is slower and reserved for avoiding lock contention, and there is no 'high performance mode' or special Salesforce-scheduled load for this.

question: Universal Containers has an integration that runs nightly to update the Product (Product2) object in Salesforce with updated product availability for over 500,000 products. The source system is green screen ERP that must be taken offline to produce nightly production reports such as the inventory availability report used for this integration. The Salesforce integration frequently reports failures due to data validation errors. Which two steps should the Technical Architect should recommend? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Ask the ERP report designers to redesign the report to provide properly formatted data.
optionB: Use SOAP headers to turn off validation rules and triggers during the integration.
optionC: Implements a regression testing policy to detect issues following development.
optionD: Review the Salesforce data model and Validation rules and modify the integration as necessary.
explanation: Frequent validation-error failures on the nightly Product load point to a mismatch between source data and Salesforce rules, so the architect should review the Salesforce data model and validation rules and adjust the integration accordingly (D), and implement a regression-testing policy to catch issues introduced by ongoing development (C). You can't turn off validation rules via SOAP headers for arbitrary loads, and redesigning the ERP report (A) doesn't address the Salesforce-side validation.

question: Northern Trail Outfitters is in the final stages of merging two Salesforce orgs but needs to keep the retiring org available for a short period of time for lead management as it is connected to multiple public web site forms. The sales department has requested that new leads are available in the new Salesforce instance within 30 minutes. Which two approaches will require the least amount of development effort? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Use the tooling API with Process Builder to insert leads in real time.
optionB: Configure named credentials in the source org.
optionC: Call the Salesforce REST API to insert the lead into the target system.
optionD: Use the Composite REST API to aggregate multiple leads in a single call.
explanation: Getting new leads from the retiring org into the new org within 30 minutes with the least development effort: configure Named Credentials in the source org to simplify authenticated callouts (B), and use the Composite REST API to insert/aggregate multiple leads in a single call (D). Tooling API (A) is for metadata, not data, and a bare single-record REST insert (C) is less efficient than the composite approach.

question: Universal Containers has a requirement to update the Salesforce Account object any time the corresponding account is updated within their financial system. Which three Salesforce capabilities should the Architect consider?
optionA: Enterprise WSDL because of a requirement to utilize SOAP-based services
optionB: Partner WSDL because of a requirement to utilize SOAP-based web services.
optionC: Partner WSDL because of a requirement to utilize REST-based web services
optionD: Partner WSDL because of a requirement to dynamically inspect field names during runtime.
optionE: Streaming API because of a requirement to dynamically inspect field names during runtime.
explanation: Updating the Salesforce Account whenever the financial system changes, via SOAP, points to the Enterprise WSDL for strongly-typed SOAP services (A), the Partner WSDL for loosely-typed SOAP web services (B), and the Partner WSDL when field names must be inspected dynamically at runtime (D). REST-based and Streaming options don't match a SOAP-based, inbound-update requirement.

question: An Architect has received a request to prevent employees that leave the company from accessing data in Salesforce after they are deactivated in the company's HR system. What should an Architect determine before recommending a solution?
optionA: Determine data volume requirements, then identify the loading schedule.
optionB: Determine inbound integration requirements, then identify frequency.
optionC: Determine data access prevention requirements, then identify system constraints.
optionD: Determine data access prevention requirements, then identify frequency.
explanation: Before recommending how to cut off access when HR deactivates an employee, the architect must first understand the inbound integration requirements from the HR system and then determine how frequently deactivation events must flow to Salesforce (B). Knowing the trigger source and timing drives whether real-time events or scheduled syncs are appropriate.

question: A company accepts payment requests 24x7. Once they accept a payment request, their service level agreement (SLA) requires them to make sure each payment request is processed by their Payment System. They track payment requests using a globally unique identifier created at the Data Entry Point. Their simplified flow is as shown in the diagram. They encounter intermittent update errors when two or more processes try to update the same Payment Request record at the same time. Which two recommendations should an integration architect make to improve their SLA and update conflict handling? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Payment System should process a payment request only once.
optionB: Data Entry Point and Middleware should automatically retry requests.
optionC: Payment System and Middleware should automatically retry requests.
optionD: Middleware should coordinate request delivery and payment processing.
explanation: To meet the SLA and handle concurrent-update conflicts on a uniquely identified payment request: ensure the Payment System processes each request exactly once, using the global identifier for idempotency (A), and have the Middleware coordinate request delivery and payment processing so two processes don't update the same record simultaneously (D). Blind automatic retries (B, C) would worsen duplicate processing without idempotency and coordination.

question: Universal Containers (UC) sends Order data to an external ERP system via ESB. UC sends an outbound message on update or Order to ESB. Once ESB completes creating the Order in the back-end ERP, it send back the Order with the Order Number from the ERP. During development, UC is experiencing an issue. When the Order is updated by ESB, it again fires a workflow rule that sends the outbound message again. Which two recommended steps can be done to prevent this looping issue?
optionA: Write an Apex trigger to send an outbound message to ESB.
optionB: Update workflow rule conditions to exclude the ERP Order Number field update.
optionC: Update workflow rule conditions to exclude the Integration User.
optionD: Update the outbound message to exclude the Integration User.
optionE: Update workflow rule conditions to exclude the ERP Order Number field update.
explanation: The outbound message fired when the ESB writes back the Order Number causes an infinite loop, so break it declaratively by updating the workflow rule conditions to exclude updates made by the Integration User (C), so the ESB's write-back doesn't re-trigger the rule. Filtering only on the Order Number field is fragile, and moving the logic into an Apex trigger doesn't address the recursion.
